1. Skill Coverage Comprehensiveness (30.1a_covered: SQL_basics, SQL_advanced, Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, Seaborn, statistics, machine_learning
1. Skill Coverage Comprehensiveness (30.1b_comment: Evaluation is based on the provided excerpt (it’s a template with many sections left as '...'). SQL shows SELECT and JOIN early plus an implied progression, but there’s no explicit coverage of GROUP BY/aggregations, subqueries/CTEs, window functions, query optimization, or interview-style SQL drills—so depth is largely unproven. Python data processing is stated (Pandas/NumPy) but not concretely broken into required core skills (I/O, cleaning, groupby/agg, merge/join, datetime, missing values, reshaping, vectorization), so only minimal credit. Visualization + statistics are stated as goals, but no explicit daily content demonstrating Matplotlib/Seaborn practice and stats coverage (distributions + hypothesis tests) beyond headings. ML introduction is mentioned, but no algorithms are specified (e.g., linear/logistic regression, trees) nor interview-relevant basics (train/test split, metrics), so score is 0.

2. Difficulty Progression Reasonableness.2a_structure_detection: 2/5 — has complete week division (9 weeks), detected 7 distinct Day(s)
2. Difficulty Progression Reasonableness.2b_comment: Starting point is mostly appropriate: beginning with SQL fundamentals (SELECT/WHERE/ORDER BY) fits an intermediate Python learner moving into data analysis, and it does not inappropriately start from Python basics or jump to ML. Weekly progression matches the recommended route (SQL -> Pandas/NumPy -> Viz/Stats -> ML -> projects) and is broadly sensible, though statistics appears only in Week 3 and could benefit from earlier light integration (e.g., summary stats during Pandas week). Intra-week/day progression cannot be fully validated because Day 3-7, Day 8-21, and Day 22-27 are not actually specified (only placeholders), so the plan does not demonstrate a clear day-by-day ramp from basics to applied tasks beyond Days 1-2; this warrants a significant deduction. Comprehensive projects are present at the end (Days 28-30) and integrate multiple skills; however, the Project 1 description includes 'purchase behavior prediction' which may be too ambitious for 2-3 days given only a light ML intro earlier, so scope/rubric should be tightened to ensure completion.

3. Resource Authenticity & Quality (20 p.3b_comment: Authenticity (4/4): The explicitly listed resources are real and well-known (freeCodeCamp YouTube SQL tutorial, W3Schools SQL pages, SQLBolt, Mode SQL Tutorial, Coursera/IBM, DataCamp, Kaggle Learn, LeetCode, HackerRank, Wes McKinney book, Ben Forta book). No obviously fabricated domains/pages appear in the excerpt. Specificity (2/4): Day 1 is fairly specific (freeCodeCamp 'first 1 hour', SQLBolt 'Lesson 1–3', W3Schools SELECT page). Day 2 includes one missing link (Programming with Mosh video not linked) and the rest of the 30-day plan is largely placeholder ('Day 3-7 continued...', 'Day 8: ...', etc.), so many recommendations lack concrete module/lesson identifiers and durations. Diversity (4/4): Strong mix of video (YouTube), reading/documentation (W3Schools/Mode), interactive practice (SQLBolt, LeetCode/HackerRank), full courses (Coursera/DataCamp/Kaggle Learn), and books.

4. Practicality & Executability (15 pts).4b_comment:
Daily practice tasks (1/4): Only Day 1 and Day 2 include clearly specified practice with concrete platforms (SQLBolt) and hands-on prompts. Most other days are placeholders ("Day 3-7 continued...", "Day 8...", "Day 15...", "Day 22...") with no explicit exercises, deliverables, or success criteria, so the plan does not actually arrange daily practice across 30 days.

Time arrangement feasibility (2/3): For the days shown, the 2.5h estimates fit the user’s 2–3h/day constraint and include a reasonable mix of video/reading/practice. However, because most days lack written task breakdowns and time estimates, overall feasibility across all 30 days cannot be verified; risk of overload is especially high in Week 3 (stats) and Week 4 (ML + projects) unless scoped tightly.

Comprehensive project quality (2/3): The projects use real sources (Kaggle, Yahoo Finance) and aim to integrate multiple skills. However, executability is under-specified: dataset links/selection, exact questions/KPIs, required outputs (notebook + slides/dashboard), and—critically—how SQL is used (local Postgres/SQLite loading + queries vs just Pandas) are not defined. The e-commerce project also mentions both "user behavior analysis" and "purchase behavior prediction" which can exceed the time available unless narrowly scoped.

Overall: As written, this is more of a plan template than an executable 30-day schedule. To reach 8–9/10 practicality, you’d need fully filled Day 3–30 entries with explicit daily exercises + deliverables and per-day time budgets, plus a clearly scoped project definition (questions, outputs, evaluation rubric, and a concrete SQL setup).
